title: Chapter 15: The Blizzard
content: The wind had a voice.

Not a metaphor. Not poetry. A voice — guttural, constant, alive — pressing against Blackwood's walls like something that wanted in. Wanted us. The weather service called it the worst blizzard in the region's recorded history. They used words like *unprecedented* and *catastrophic*. I lay in my dormitory bed at 5 a.m. listening to it scream through the gap beneath the window frame, and I thought: *I know something else that sounds like that.*
Me. Three weeks ago. The night I said yes to Julian.

Classes were cancelled. The administration sent a campus-wide alert confining all students to interior spaces. The dining hall shuttered. The library locked. Even the chapel — that drafty, beautiful mausoleum — went dark. Blackwood sealed itself like a body fighting infection.

I couldn't stay in that room. The walls were too close and Julian's ring on my finger was too heavy and the wind sounded too much like my own voice, so I pulled on my coat and went to the only place that still felt like it belonged to me.
The conservatory door was unlocked. I should have known.
Heath stood in the dark between the orchid beds, and the first thing I saw was his hands.

Blood on his knuckles. Fresh. Still wet, gleaming faintly in the grey light that filtered through the glass roof — what little light there was, the sky outside a swollen bruise of cloud. He must have been at the chapel wall again. Punched it until something gave. I could see the damage even from the doorway: split skin across two fingers on his right hand, the left worse, raw and open and hanging.

He didn't look at me when I came in. He was staring at the orchids — the dying ones in the far bed, the ones the frost had crept in and killed while the heating system failed. Their petals were brown and crinkled, limp on their stems. The smell hit me as I stepped closer: sweet rot, vegetal and close, like grief metabolizing itself into compost.
His eyes were red. Not from the cold. Not from the dark. From something that happened in the hours before I found him here, standing alone among the corpses of flowers.
"How long have you been here?" I asked.
"Long enough."

His voice was sandpaper. Torn. I'd heard him pissed off. I'd heard him careful, controlled, the Heath who built walls out of syllables and leaned against them like they were load-bearing. This wasn't that voice. This was the voice underneath. The one he never let anyone hear.

I wanted to go to him. Every cell in my body angled toward him like iron filings toward a magnet. But I was wearing Julian's ring and I'd come from a building where Julian's cologne still clung to the hallway outside my door, and I wasn't free to move through space the way I wanted to anymore. I hadn't been free since I said yes.
"Heath —"
"Does he touch you?"
The question hit like a hand across the face.

He turned. Finally looked at me. And his eyes — God, his eyes. Wrecked. Open in a way I'd never seen them, not even in the dark of the chapel loft, not even when he kissed me like I was oxygen. Every wall he'd built in the last three weeks was rubble at his feet.
"Does he hold you at night?" He stepped closer. Not touching. Closer. "Do you let him?"
"It's not like that —"

"Then what's it like?" His voice cracked. Not dramatically. Quietly. The way ice cracks under weight it can't bear. "Tell me, Cat. Tell me what it's like to wear his ring and come to our place smelling like his cologne."
I opened my mouth. Nothing came out.

Because what could I say? *He touches me and I let him because it's the price?* *He holds my hand at dinner while his father's lawyers draft the contracts that will save my family?* *I wear his ring like a tourniquet — tight enough to stop the bleeding, and if it kills the hand, well, at least the patient survives?*
"It's strategy," I said. The word tasted like ash even as it left my tongue.
Heath laughed. Once. A sound without humor, hollow as a drum.
"Strategy." He repeated it like he was learning a foreign language. "You're telling me it's *strategy* when I can smell him on you from three feet away?"

I couldn't move. Couldn't breathe. The wind shrieked outside and the conservatory glass rattled in its frame, and I could hear the ghost of our parallel lines scored into the stone planter behind me — two marks, one story, a pact made when the summer was still warm and nothing between us was broken yet.
"Do you love him?"
The question came so quiet I almost missed it. Almost pretended I had.
"No —"
"Do you love *me*?"

And there it was. The impossible question. The one I couldn't answer cleanly because the truth was a rat's nest of want and duty and fear, and if I pulled one thread the whole thing would unravel, and I couldn't afford to unravel, I couldn't afford to be the girl who chose wrong, I couldn't afford to lose the only hand I'd been dealt that had any power in it at all.
"I have to do this," I said. My voice was shaking. "If I don't, he'll destroy you. He'll take your scholarship, he'll revoke your visa — he told me, Heath. He told me what he'd do, and I —"
"I don't care about the scholarship!"

He was shouting now. Shouting in the quiet dark of the conservatory with the dying orchids and the blood on his hands and the wind trying to rip the roof off above us, and the sound of him — the raw, unprocessed pain of it — cut through me cleaner than any knife.
"I don't care about the visa! I don't care about any of it!" He stepped back, chest heaving. "I care about *you*. I care about you choosing someone else over us. Over *this*."

And somewhere in the tangle of everything I was feeling, my brain translated his words wrong. I heard: *You're not enough.* I heard: *Your strategy is a failure.* I heard: *Even when you sacrifice, even when you bleed, even when you do the hard thing that no one else will do — it isn't enough.*

And he — somewhere in the tangle of everything he was feeling — heard me wrong too. I know he did. He heard: *I'm choosing him.* He heard: *Julian is what I want.* He heard: *You were never going to be enough for me to risk it.*
Both of us wrong. Both of us devastated. Both of us standing in the dark with the truth between us like a body neither of us could bear to look at.
The wind screamed.

Heath stared at me. A long time. Long enough that I could see his throat work, see him swallow whatever was left of the words he might have said. The conservatory glass shuddered. A pane somewhere near the roof groaned, flexed. The orchids on the far bench released another waft of their dying sweetness — cloying, suffocating, the smell of something beautiful rotting in place.
"Then do it alone."
Four words. Very quiet. The quietest he'd spoken all night.
He turned.
He walked toward the conservatory door.

And my body — my stupid, traitorous, wanting body — moved before my mind could stop it. One step. Just one. My foot lifted and fell and the distance between us shrank by three feet and my hand started to lift — and then stopped. Frozen midair. Because strategy. Because guilt. Because that cold, calculating part of me that had kept me alive through my mother's disappearance and my father's ruin and every Vance catastrophe since the day I was born whispered: *If you chase him, you lose everything Julian gives you. If you chase him, there is no protection. If you chase him, you are a girl with nothing.*
I was so used to choosing power over want that I couldn't recognize the moment when want should win.
My hand dropped.
Heath reached the door. His fingers — the bloody ones, the torn ones — closed around the handle. He pulled it open.
And the blizzard came in.

I've never felt cold like that. It wasn't weather. It was violence. The wind hit my face like a wall of ice and I staggered back, eyes streaming, the frost crystallizing on my lashes so fast I had to blink and blink to keep them from sealing shut. The world beyond the door was white. Pure, obliterating white. Not snow — absence. A negation of everything that wasn't freezing and lethal and final.
Heath stepped into it.

Not running. Not hesitating. Walking. One foot, then the other, shoulders square, head up. Like a man choosing the cold over a long slow death by watching the person he loves belong to someone else. Like dignity was the only thing the storm couldn't strip from him. Like the bravest, stupidest, most heartbreaking choice he'd ever made was also the only one left.
The snow swallowed him in three steps.

Three. One — his silhouette dark against the whiteout. Two — the shape of him blurring, losing edges. Three — nothing. Just wind. Just white. Just the howl of something that wanted in filling the space where he'd been.
I stood in the doorway.

The cold bit through my coat, my sweater, my skin. It found the spaces between my ribs and burrowed in. My fingers went numb. My lips cracked. The frost on my eyelashes turned my vision into a kaleidoscope of ice and I kept blinking, kept watching the white, kept waiting for a shape to reappear, for him to turn around, for the universe to be kind enough to make him come back so I wouldn't have to be the one to go after him.
The universe is never that kind.
One minute.
Two.
Three.

The cold was a living thing now, gnawing at me from every direction. The wind screamed through the open door and I heard it — really heard it, the way it layered over itself, harmonics of fury, a frequency that resonated in my chest like a second heartbeat. *You let him go,* the wind said. *You let him go and you're standing here and you're not going after him and this is who you are — a girl in a doorway, watching.*
I stepped back inside. I closed the door.

My hands were shaking so badly it took three tries to seat the latch. When it finally caught, the silence was worse than the wind. The conservatory held its breath. The orchids were dead and rotting and still, and the grey light through the glass had turned the color of old bone, and the planter was there — our planter, the one with the two parallel lines scored into the stone, mine and his, never touching, never crossing, running side by side forever.

I sat down on the planter's edge. The stone was cold through my clothes. I could taste the cold, metallic and mineral, like licking a frozen coin. My body was starting to shiver uncontrollably, teeth chattering, muscles spasming, and I let it because at least the shaking was something. At least it was movement.
I pressed my thumb into the pact scar on my palm.

Pressed hard. The skin was thin there — it had always been thin, ever since the night we'd cut our hands and pressed them together in the dark and promised — what? That we'd choose each other? That we'd never let anything come between us? Some promise. Some pact. The skin had healed ugly, a raised ridge of scar tissue that ached when the weather changed, and I pressed my thumb into it now, grinding down until I felt the tissue split.

Blood welled. Warm. Almost hot against the cold of my palm. I lifted my hand and looked at it — the dark spread of it in the grey light, the way it filled the creases of my palm like water finding the lowest point. I touched my tongue to it without thinking. Copper and salt and iron. The taste of damage. The taste of him, once, when he'd done the same thing in the dark of the chapel loft and we'd laughed about it, mouths stained red, tasting each other's blood like it was communion wine.
I didn't wipe it.

I let it pool. Let it drip onto the stone planter between the parallel lines, filling the groove of my line with red. I watched it until the bleeding slowed, until the cold did its work and clotted the wound, until my palm was a mess of dried copper and fresh hurt.

I should grieve. I knew that. Somewhere in the architecture of being human, there was a room for grief, and I should walk into it and let myself fall apart. But I'd buried that right years ago. I'd built a floor over it and danced on top and told myself I was fine, I was strong, I was a Vance and Vances don't crumble. I'd walled off the part of me that knew how to cry for the things I'd lost because there was always another loss coming, always another thing to survive, and you can't afford to grieve what's gone when tomorrow's catastrophe is already at the door.
So I didn't cry.

I sat on the planter with my bloody palm turned upward and the smell of dying orchids in my nose and the taste of my own blood on my tongue and the howl of the blizzard filling every corner of the glass room until it was inside me, until I was made of it, until there was nothing left but cold and noise and the knowledge that he was out there somewhere in that white void and I was in here, in the dark, alone.
He'd said *then do it alone*.
So I was.

I sat there. The wind screamed. The glass rattled. The frost crept across the orchid blooms in fractal patterns of destruction, consuming what the cold hadn't already killed, and I watched it with dry eyes and a bleeding palm and a silence inside me so total it had its own gravity.
I did not move.
I did not move.
